past_medical_history: Hypertension for 10 years, currently on amlodipine and candesartan
Left-eye anisometropic amblyopia since childhood with best corrected visual acuity of 0.08 in the left eye
Mild dyslipidemia diagnosed 4 years ago, managed with dietary modification
No prior neurological diagnoses or hospitalizations
family_history: Father deceased at age 78 from ischemic stroke
Mother alive at age 84 with type 2 diabetes
No family history of autoimmune or neurological diseases

social_history.alcohol: Rare social drinker, less than once per month
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a single-story home

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech fluent without dysarthria. Language comprehension and repetition intact. Attention and concentration preserved. No cognitive deficits on bedside testing. Cooperative and appropriate affect.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual acuity 0.8 (6/7.5) in the right eye, 0.08 (6/75) in the left eye (longstanding amblyopia). Pupils equal and reactive to light; no relative afferent pupillary defect. Fundoscopy shows bilateral myopic changes with sharp optic disc margins; no papilledema. CN III, IV, VI: Primary gaze orthotropic. On attempted left lateral gaze, the right eye fails to adduct past midline and the left eye fails to fully abduct, consistent with bilateral horizontal gaze palsy. No nystagmus. Red glass testing reveals uncrossed diplopia. Vertical eye movements are full. CN V: Facial sensation intact to light touch and pinprick in all three divisions bilaterally. CN VII: Left lower motor neuron facial nerve palsy with incomplete left eyelid closure, inability to raise left eyebrow, flattening of left nasolabial fold, and forehead involvement (indicating peripheral pattern). CN VIII-XII: Hearing intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally.
neurological_exam.motor: Upper extremities: 5/5 bilaterally in deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, grip, and interossei. Lower extremities: 5/5 bilaterally in hip flexors, knee extensors, ankle dorsiflexors, and plantar flexors. No pronator drift. No fasciculations or atrophy. Tone normal throughout.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception intact in all extremities. No dermatomal sensory level. No truncal sensory deficits.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally. Triceps 2+ bilaterally. Brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar 2+ bilaterally. Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing intact bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements normal.
neurological_exam.gait: Gait steady and narrow-based. Tandem gait performed without difficulty. Romberg sign negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No meningismus. No Lhermitte sign. The combination of bilateral horizontal gaze palsy and ipsilateral peripheral facial nerve palsy localizes to the left dorsal pons, involving the paramedian pontine reticular formation and the facial nerve nucleus or fascicle.

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden-onset double vision and left facial weakness for 1 week
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Hartono is a 61-year-old right-handed man of Indonesian descent who presents with a one-week history of sudden-onset binocular horizontal diplopia and left facial muscle weakness. He reports that approximately seven days ago he awoke in the morning and immediately noticed that he was seeing two images side by side when looking to the left. The diplopia is constant, worsens on leftward gaze, and resolves when he closes either eye. He has compensated by turning his head to the right to avoid the double vision. On the same day he also noticed weakness on the left side of his face, with difficulty closing his left eye fully and an asymmetric smile. His wife confirmed that the left side of his face appeared droopy. He denies any change in visual acuity in either eye beyond his known longstanding left-eye amblyopia. He specifically denies any headache, vertigo, hearing loss, dysphagia, dysarthria, limb weakness, limb numbness, gait difficulty, bowel or bladder dysfunction, or recent head trauma. He has not experienced any prior episodes of neurological symptoms, including numbness, tingling, visual loss, or motor weakness. He has no history of fever, night sweats, weight loss, joint pain, rash, or oral ulcers. His blood pressure has been reasonably controlled on his current regimen, and he has been compliant with his medications. His primary care physician initially suspected a possible lacunar stroke given his age and vascular risk factors, and he was referred for urgent neurological evaluation. On further questioning, he mentions that he has noticed mild generalized fatigue over the past two to three months, which he attributed to poor sleep quality, and occasional mild tingling in his fingertips bilaterally that he dismissed as being related to gardening activities. These symptoms were not brought to medical attention previously.

findings.location: Periventricular white matter, bilateral, extending perpendicular to the lateral ventricles
findings.size: Multiple lesions ranging from 4 mm to 15 mm

signal_characteristics.T1: Several lesions hypointense (T1 black holes indicating chronic demyelination)
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense, oriented perpendicular to the ventricles (Dawson fingers pattern)

findings.location: Cortical and juxtacortical white matter, bilateral cerebellar hemispheres

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Post-contrast enhancement present in two lesions

findings.location: Left paramedian pontine reticular formation, dorsal pons

signal_characteristics.DWI: Hyperintense due to T2 shine-through effect
signal_characteristics.ADC: Normal (no restricted diffusion, excluding acute ischemia)

findings.location: Right juxtacortical white matter, frontal lobe

additional_observations: DWI hyperintensity in the pontine lesion is attributable to T2 shine-through, confirmed by normal ADC maps
No evidence of hemorrhage on susceptibility-weighted imaging
No mass lesion or neoplastic features
Mild small vessel ischemic changes in the deep white matter appropriate for age, distinct from demyelinating lesions
mri.impression: Multiple demyelinating lesions involving the periventricular white matter (Dawson fingers pattern), juxtacortical regions, and infratentorial structures including a lesion in the left dorsal pons at the paramedian pontine reticular formation that correlates with the patient's bilateral horizontal gaze palsy and left facial nerve palsy. Multiple lesions demonstrate gadolinium enhancement, indicating active inflammation. The simultaneous presence of enhancing and non-enhancing lesions satisfies the 2017 McDonald criteria for dissemination in space (periventricular, juxtacortical, and infratentorial involvement) and dissemination in time. DWI hyperintensity in the pontine lesion is due to T2 shine-through and does not represent acute ischemic stroke. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Mild left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (Sokolow-Lyon 38 mm)
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 74 bpm with mild LVH by voltage criteria, likely related to chronic hypertension. No arrhythmia or ischemic changes. No cardiac source of embolism identified.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Total cholesterol 212 mg/dL (H), LDL cholesterol 134 mg/dL (H), Triglycerides 152 mg/dL (H), Vitamin D, 25-hydroxy 18 ng/mL (L).

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive — 5 bands unique to CSF, not present in serum

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 15 cmH2O. WBC: 12 cells/microL (reference: 0-5) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 48 mg/dL (reference: 15-45 mg/dL). Glucose: 58 mg/dL (reference: 40-70 mg/dL) (ratio 0.59 (serum glucose 98 mg/dL)). Oligoclonal bands: Positive — 5 bands unique to CSF, not present in serum. IgG index: 0.78 (elevated; reference: <0.70). IgG synthesis rate: 10.8 mg/day (elevated; reference: <8.0 mg/day). Myelin basic protein: 5.2 ng/mL (mildly elevated; reference: <4.0 ng/mL). Cytology: No malignant cells. VDRL: Nonreactive.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Right eye P100 latency 112 ms ms (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Right eye P100 latency: 112 ms (mildly prolonged, reference <102 ms) (H)

findings.location: Cervical spinal cord at C5-C6, dorsolateral columns, left-sided
findings.size: Less than one vertebral segment in length

signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense, involving less than half the cross-sectional area of the cord

additional_observations: Mild degenerative disc disease at C5-C6 and C6-C7, without significant cord compression
Normal conus medullaris and cauda equina
output.impression: Single short-segment cervical spinal cord lesion at C5-C6 (non-enhancing, chronic), with characteristics typical of demyelinating disease. Mild cervical spondylosis noted incidentally without cord compression. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.size: Multiple lesions, unchanged compared to prior study

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: No enhancement (previously enhancing cerebellar lesions no longer enhance)

findings.location: Left dorsal pons (paramedian pontine reticular formation)
Left periventricular white matter, anterior horn of lateral ventricle

volumetrics.resolving_lesions: 3 previously enhancing lesions no longer enhance

additional_observations: Resolution of enhancement in previously active lesions
No new spinal cord lesions on sagittal cervical images
No evidence of progressive brain atrophy
output.impression: Follow-up MRI at 3 months shows one new small enhancing lesion in the left periventricular white matter, indicating ongoing subclinical disease activity. Previously enhancing lesions have resolved. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: late-onset multiple sclerosis diagnosis over age 50

results.title: Late-onset multiple sclerosis: clinical features, prognostic factors, and outcomes
results.authors: Polliack ML, Barak Y, Achiron A
results.key_findings: Late-onset MS (>50 years) accounts for 4-9.6% of MS cases. Primary progressive course is more common but relapsing-remitting can occur. Higher proportion of males compared to typical MS. Motor and cerebellar symptoms are more common at onset. Diagnosis is often delayed due to lower clinical suspicion.

results.title: Multiple sclerosis in Southeast Asia: a systematic review
results.key_findings: MS prevalence in Southeast Asia is lower than in Western countries but increasing with improved diagnostic awareness. Late-onset presentations may be more common in regions with historically low MS recognition. MRI and CSF oligoclonal bands remain key diagnostic tools.

output.summary: Late-onset multiple sclerosis (LOMS, diagnosis after age 50) represents approximately 4-10% of MS cases. Compared to typical-onset MS, LOMS shows a higher male-to-female ratio, more frequent motor and cerebellar presentations, a greater proportion of primary progressive course, and often delayed diagnosis due to vascular disease mimics. In Southeast Asian populations, MS prevalence is lower but diagnostic recognition is improving. The 2017 McDonald criteria apply regardless of age, and MRI with Dawson fingers pattern and CSF oligoclonal bands remain diagnostic cornerstones.

output.proposed: Ocrelizumab 300 mg IV infusion every 6 months
interactions: Concurrent use with amlodipine: No significant interaction
Concurrent use with candesartan: No significant interaction
Concurrent use with aspirin 81 mg: No significant interaction; aspirin does not affect B-cell depletion efficacy
contraindications: Active hepatitis B infection (must screen HBsAg and anti-HBc before initiation)
Active severe infection — defer until resolved
warnings: Screen for hepatitis B and tuberculosis before initiation
Increased risk of infections due to B-cell depletion; monitor for respiratory and urinary tract infections
Infusion-related reactions common with first dose; premedicate with methylprednisolone, diphenhydramine, and acetaminophen
Ensure vaccinations (including influenza and pneumococcal) are up to date before starting; avoid live vaccines during treatment
Monitor immunoglobulin levels periodically; prolonged use may lead to hypogammaglobulinemia
Age-related considerations: older patients may have higher infection risk
alternatives: Dimethyl fumarate 240 mg twice daily (oral, moderate efficacy)
Natalizumab 300 mg IV monthly (high efficacy, requires JCV monitoring)
Glatiramer acetate 40 mg SC three times weekly (lower efficacy, favorable safety in older adults)
Cladribine 3.5 mg/kg cumulative over 2 years (oral, immune reconstitution therapy)

results.title: Diagnosis of multiple sclerosis: 2017 revisions of the McDonald criteria
results.authors: Thompson AJ, Banwell BL, Barkhof F, et al.
results.key_findings: Revised criteria allow CSF oligoclonal bands to substitute for dissemination in time. Symptomatic and asymptomatic MRI lesions can both be used. Cortical lesions can substitute for juxtacortical lesions.

results.title: MRI criteria for the diagnosis of multiple sclerosis: MAGNIMS consensus guidelines
results.authors: Filippi M, Rocca MA, Ciccarelli O, et al.
results.key_findings: Dissemination in space requires at least 1 T2 lesion in at least 2 of 4 CNS areas: periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord.

output.summary: The 2017 McDonald criteria for MS diagnosis require demonstration of dissemination in space (DIS) and dissemination in time (DIT). DIS requires one or more T2 lesions in at least 2 of 4 characteristic locations (periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord). DIT can be demonstrated by simultaneous presence of enhancing and non-enhancing lesions, new T2/enhancing lesions on follow-up MRI, or CSF-specific oligoclonal bands. In this patient, DIS is met by lesions in periventricular, juxtacortical, and infratentorial regions. DIT is met by the simultaneous presence of enhancing and non-enhancing lesions, and also independently by CSF oligoclonal bands.

differential.key_features: Age and hypertension raise suspicion, but DWI hyperintensity is due to T2 shine-through with normal ADC maps, effectively excluding acute ischemia. Additionally, the multiple periventricular Dawson fingers lesions are not consistent with vascular disease.
No evidence of hemorrhage on SWI or CT. Horizontal gaze palsy can be caused by hemorrhagic pontine lesions but imaging excludes this.
Small pontine lesion without mass effect, no expansion of the pons, and multiple additional lesions in typical MS distribution make neoplasm unlikely.
AQP4 antibodies negative. Periventricular Dawson fingers pattern is typical of MS, not NMOSD. No longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis. No optic neuritis in this presentation.
Normal ESR/CRP, negative ANA. Dawson fingers morphology with periventricular perpendicular orientation is characteristic of MS, not vasculitis.

differential.diagnosis: Isolated seventh nerve palsy (Bell palsy) with coincidental vascular lesion
differential.key_features: No separate facial nerve nucleus lesion on MRI; the single pontine lesion accounts for both the horizontal gaze palsy and the facial nerve palsy anatomically.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history including symptom onset, temporal profile, and any prior neurological symptoms that might indicate earlier demyelinating events
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Sudden-onset diplopia and facial weakness over 1 week, with subtle prior symptoms (fatigue, fingertip tingling) that may represent subclinical demyelination

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination localizing the lesion to the brainstem
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral horizontal gaze palsy and left peripheral facial nerve palsy localizing to the left dorsal pons (PPRF and facial nerve nucleus/fascicle)

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI brain with gadolinium contrast, including DWI/ADC sequences to differentiate ischemic stroke from demyelination
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Multiple demyelinating lesions with Dawson fingers pattern, enhancing pontine lesion with T2 shine-through (not restricted diffusion), satisfying McDonald criteria for DIS and DIT

optimal_actions.action: Order basic labs including CBC, BMP, glucose, lipid panel, ESR, CRP, ANA, vitamin B12, NMO-IgG, and MOG antibodies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal CBC and BMP, mild dyslipidemia, normal inflammatory markers, negative AQP4 and MOG antibodies, low vitamin D

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with CSF analysis including oligoclonal bands, IgG index, and VDRL
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Oligoclonal bands present (5 bands), elevated IgG index, mild lymphocytic pleocytosis, negative VDRL

optimal_actions.action: Obtain ECG to evaluate for cardiac arrhythmia as a potential embolic source (given vascular risk factors)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal sinus rhythm with mild LVH; no atrial fibrillation

optimal_actions.action: Initiate IV methylprednisolone 1g daily for 3-5 days for acute demyelinating relapse
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Expected improvement in diplopia and facial weakness over days to weeks

optimal_actions.action: Discuss diagnosis of relapsing-remitting MS with patient and counsel on prognosis and disease-modifying therapy options
Initiate disease-modifying therapy, considering higher-efficacy options given active inflammation and late-onset presentation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Selected DMT appropriate for age and comorbidities (e.g., ocrelizumab with hepatitis B screening)

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI cervical and thoracic spine with contrast to complete disease burden assessment
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Short-segment cervical spinal cord lesion at C5-C6, consistent with MS

optimal_actions.action: Supplement vitamin D given low levels
Schedule follow-up MRI in 3 months to establish treatment baseline

critical_actions: Obtain brain MRI with gadolinium and DWI/ADC to distinguish demyelination from acute ischemic stroke
Confirm absence of restricted diffusion on ADC maps to exclude pontine infarct
Perform lumbar puncture to assess for oligoclonal bands and elevated IgG index
Check NMO-IgG (aquaporin-4 antibodies) and MOG antibodies to exclude alternative diagnoses
Treat acute relapse with IV methylprednisolone
Initiate disease-modifying therapy after confirming MS diagnosis
contraindicated_actions: Do not administer IV tPA or thrombolytics — this is not an acute ischemic stroke despite initial suspicion
Do not start chronic oral corticosteroids as maintenance therapy for MS
Do not perform brain biopsy — diagnosis is achievable with non-invasive McDonald criteria
Do not delay disease-modifying therapy while waiting for spontaneous remission
Do not initiate natalizumab without first checking JC virus antibody status
Do not discontinue antihypertensive medications during workup
key_reasoning_points: The combination of bilateral horizontal gaze palsy and ipsilateral peripheral facial nerve palsy localizes to the left dorsal pons, specifically the paramedian pontine reticular formation and facial nerve nucleus or fascicle

Acute ischemic stroke must be excluded given the patient's age and vascular risk factors — DWI hyperintensity due to T2 shine-through (not restricted diffusion) on normal ADC maps effectively rules out acute ischemia
Periventricular lesions perpendicular to the ventricles (Dawson fingers) are pathognomonic for demyelinating disease and not seen in vascular white matter disease
The simultaneous presence of enhancing and non-enhancing lesions on a single MRI satisfies dissemination in time under 2017 McDonald criteria
Dissemination in space is met by lesions in periventricular, juxtacortical, and infratentorial locations
CSF oligoclonal bands provide independent evidence of intrathecal immune activation and can substitute for DIT under 2017 McDonald criteria
The absence of a separate facial nerve nucleus lesion on MRI excludes the possibility of coincidental isolated Bell palsy
Late-onset MS (over age 50) is uncommon but well-documented, with a higher male-to-female ratio and more frequent motor/brainstem presentations compared to typical-onset MS
Negative AQP4 and MOG antibodies are essential to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD, which require different treatment approaches

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward case with classic MRI findings (Dawson fingers, enhancing lesions meeting McDonald criteria) and positive CSF oligoclonal bands. The main diagnostic challenge is the atypical demographics (older male) raising initial suspicion for stroke, but DWI/ADC findings and characteristic lesion distribution clearly point to MS.
